results.title: PPP and Gilgit-Baltistan
results.description: Gilgit-Baltistan is no ordinary land. It has long stood at the crossroads of civilisations, where the world’s mightiest mountain ranges converge in breathtaking silence. Marco Polo crossed its ancient passes in wonder; Lord Curzon read its horizons with the eyes of empire. The explorers of the nineteenth century left it not merely mapped, but mythologised—naming it the Gateway to India, the place Where Three Empires Meet, and the Cockpit of Asia. Gilgit-Baltistan, in every sense, is a key that opens several doors at once. It was Shaheed Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto who understood this truth with rare clarity. Among his earliest acts in power was a visit to the Northern Areas in 1972—a journey that was not ceremonial, but transformative. He renamed the Resident as Commissioner and the Political Agent as Deputy Commissioner, dismantling the colonial architecture of governance. The feudal order of Jagirdars, Meers, and Rajas was abolished, sweeping away centuries of inherited inequality. On 3 July 1975, the Northern Areas Council Legal Framework Order was enacted—the first codified legal framework in the region’s modern history—while the Princely State of Hunza was dissolved and integrated into Gilgit-Baltistan. Knowing that political rights alone cannot sustain a people, he also ensured subsidised wheat and flour reached the poorest households in the region. These reforms did not merely alter governance; they restored dignity to a people who had long remained on the margins of the state. Rs570m grant approved for small industrial estate infrastructure restoration When Shaheed Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto was martyred, Mohtarma Benazir Bhutto carried the torch forward. In 1994, her government replaced the Northern Areas Council with the Northern Areas Legislative Council, laying the foundations of representative governance in a region that had been denied political participation for decades. She believed that the people of Gilgit-Baltistan deserved institutions through which they could shape their own future. Her commitment was not confined to constitutional reforms alone. During the Siachen conflict, Benazir Bhutto travelled to the front lines, standing beside Pakistan’s soldiers amidst the towering peaks that had always held strategic and emotional significance for her. It was a powerful demonstration that the mountains of Gilgit-Baltistan were not a distant frontier, but an inseparable part of Pakistan’s national identity. Collectorate of Customs (Enforcement), Hyderabad records strong operational performance Then came President Asif Ali Zardari, under whose leadership the most consequential constitutional transformation of the region took place. In 2009, the Pakistan Peoples Party government introduced the Gilgit-Baltistan Empowerment and Self-Governance Order, fundamentally reshaping the political landscape of the region. For the first time in history, Gilgit-Baltistan was granted a province-like governance structure with its own elected Chief Minister and Governor. The Legislative Assembly received authority to legislate on key sectors including hydropower, minerals, tourism, health, and education, while the Chief Court was elevated to the status of a High Court, strengthening the administration of justice. Simultaneously, major development initiatives such as the Diamer-Bhasha Dam, the Satpara Dam, and the widening of the Skardu-Gilgit Road reflected the PPP’s commitment to pairing political empowerment with economic progress. Gold price falls by Rs1,100 per tola The journey, however, did not end with self-governance. Under the renewed leadership of President Asif Ali Zardari and Chairman Bilawal Bhutto Zardari, the Pakistan Peoples Party has continued to pursue the constitutional mainstreaming of Gilgit-Baltistan with the same conviction that has defined its history. The vision articulated by Chairman Bilawal Bhutto Zardari is clear: the people of Gilgit-Baltistan deserve not only administrative empowerment but full constitutional recognition, meaningful representation within Pakistan’s parliamentary system, and an equal voice in shaping the federation’s future. The PPP has consistently advocated granting the region constitutional provincial status while safeguarding Pakistan’s principled position on the Jammu and Kashmir dispute. Equally significant has been the party’s unwavering commitment to democratic representation. Rather than perpetuating politics rooted in inherited privilege or feudal influence, the Pakistan Peoples Party stood firmly behind a leadership that emerged from the mandate of the people. The nomination of a Chief Minister who truly represented the aspirations of ordinary citizens reaffirmed the party’s belief that democracy derives its legitimacy from the ballot box alone. This philosophy was equally evident during the election campaign, when senior party leaders, including Governor Punjab Sardar Saleem Haider Khan, spent days travelling across Gilgit-Baltistan, engaging communities, building consensus, and persuading political opponents through dialogue to join hands with the Pakistan Peoples Party. It reflected the Bhutto legacy that national integration is achieved through democratic participation, inclusion, and trust in the wisdom of the people. Pakistan prioritising port modernisation, blue economy, sustainability, says Junaid Anwar Today, under President Asif Ali Zardari’s leadership, the Pakistan Peoples Party continues to reaffirm its commitment to the constitutional future of Gilgit-Baltistan. The party remains steadfast in its resolve that the people of this strategically vital region should enjoy equal constitutional rights, stronger democratic institutions, and meaningful participation in the federation while preserving Pakistan’s principled stance on the Jammu and Kashmir dispute. This is not a departure from the Bhutto vision; it is its natural continuation—a vision that has consistently sought to replace exclusion with inclusion, uncertainty with constitutional clarity, and neglect with national ownership. IEEEP, Badar Expo Solutions hold soft launch of 15th edition of IEEEP Fair 2026 If one surveys the history of Gilgit-Baltistan since 1947 with honest eyes, a single truth emerges: every major milestone in the region’s political empowerment bears the imprint of the Pakistan Peoples Party. From dismantling the remnants of feudal rule to establishing representative institutions, from self-governance to the continuing pursuit of constitutional mainstreaming, the PPP has remained the foremost advocate for the people of Gilgit-Baltistan. The bond between the Bhutto family and the people of these mountains is not merely political—it is historical, enduring, and forged through decades of reforms, representation, and democratic struggle. The story of Gilgit-Baltistan’s empowerment is, in many respects, inseparable from the story of the Pakistan Peoples Party itself. Long live the people of Gilgit-Baltistan. Long live the Pakistan Peoples Party. Sardar Faizan Haider KhanThe writer is a politician from NA 50 Attock Fateh Jang.

results.title: Police Ban Tourist Entry To Pandavkada, Taloja Hill & Other Monsoon Hotspots In Kharghar To Prevent Deaths
results.description: Police have imposed a complete ban on tourist entry to several monsoon hotspots in Kharghar and Taloja, including Pandavkada Waterfall and Owe Camp Dam, under Section 163 of the BNSS, 2026. The move aims to prevent drowning deaths and accidents during heavy rains. Authorities said strict legal action will be taken against violators entering restricted hazardous zones.
